sentences.text: Sure.
We are going toward Darnice, and going on a temporary bridge over the [NON-ENGLISH].
We see hundreds and hundreds of labor campers like we, from other units, are working there, being tortured, being abused there because we were sitting on that bridge and watching it.
And we started to see the taste of a labor camp.
We arrive to Darnice.
And in Darnice, about 30 of us, a small group, that were assigned-- there was an animal hospital for horses, horses who were sick or something.
Was a hospital for horses.
And they needed some help, some labor there.
So we were assigned to these horses.
Now, I never worked with horse.
I didn't know these horses were bad horses, were kicking, and were so-- people struggled there.
We had different guards, and different officers there, not from our unit.
And the 30 people there, we had very big, bad experience.
First suicide among us was one man, by the name of [NON-ENGLISH], he committed suicide there.
Later on, I became there a barber, a laundryman, different positions there, struggled also.
But this boy, who was with me-- he was, what you call it-- a sergeant.
I was some under his protection.
And I had a little better food, because all his leftovers, I ate that.
So being a laundryman, and we were there for a short time.
I don't know what reason.
It was exactly before Yom Kippur, and a big explosion was there.
The Russians left there some mines.
And it was a chain explosion.
After this explosion, we stayed there quite a few days.
I don't know what happened.
The horses got wild.
They ran away.
And the explosions were there.
We went back to our unit.
And we went back our unit.
And our unit wasn't anywhere in Kiev.
We went back to Fastiv.
Fastiv is a small town not far from Kiev.
It's also a crossroad town.
And over there, we were assigned to work in-- in forest.
This forest-- what did we do there?
To cut wood for-- to a certain size, to-- which they're making carriages.
This will be delivered to Kiev.
And the officers, the Hungarians needed their-- a lot of understanding.
They were among Ukrainians.
They were among the Germans.
Germans were also there.
And they needed someone who can understand this language.

Since I spoke Hungarian and German, and they were asking if one of us spoke those language, Hungarian, German, and Russian, they took me to this-- as an interpreter from one day to another, so I didn't have to work so hard.
I came back already.
These guards looked at me as a different person.
And I had a little bit better than the rest of them.
But the Hungarian guards and the Hungarian officers who were our commanders abused us constantly, beatings, the same thing as before.
We didn't have no communication whatsoever with our families.
We weren't allowed to write, and who was writing there.
Under certain occasion and circumstances, some people managed to write, to smuggle over a letter to an officer, and then when went home or so.
I-- happened that we lived in a place-- I believe it was before a synagogue there-- but it was during that time before World War I, because looked like a synagogue.
Nearby lived some peasants.
And as an interpreter, I had to do some interpretation for an officer.
From time to time, I ran into this peasant.
There was a woman-- she had a child.
Her name was Herman.
And she just told me what happened to Jews in Kiev and in Fastiv-- they killed all the Jews, and what happened, and all these graves.
I still couldn't understand.
But this was the first time I found out about this massive, massive destroying of Jews.
She said, no Jew was left here.
And if they lived, they are there in hiding.
She had a little boy by the name of also Herman-- no one knew that he is Jewish.
Because only her husband was Jewish, and he ran away with the Soviet soldiers.
So we knew a little bit here what's happening.
None of us wanted to believe.
We didn't encounter any Jews there.
So we stayed there in this Fastiv for quite one year almost, working on this forest.
We came there-- And so you were an interpreter for a year?
No, I wasn't-- I was occasionally, I was interpreter.
Then I worked also.
But then, happened a whole story.
These who were also very hard and harsh to us, went home on vacation-- the guards.
And they smuggled over some letters to the parents who were there.
And they found out-- my uncles were very influential people from the past, like those who were involved with politics, very old Jews.
Their parents were, you know, were with [NON-ENGLISH].
And they found out about us.
And they started to bribe them, these guards.
They went on vacation.
And these guards, when they came back, brought some presents, some watches.
And besides, they did ask money from them.
And they became completely different.
The guards, until now, they didn't know there is such a bribery.
Now they found out there could be bribery.
And it became much easier.
As a matter of fact, our officer, as a matter of fact, took one of the doctors.
We had nine doctors in our unit.
OK, imagine, nine doctors-- we didn't need nine doctors.
He took one of the doctors and just put them in a Hungarian clothes, and brought them back home to Hungary.
Then he took the last-- one Hungarian officer who was a commander of ours, he was bribed.
And he took them back to Hungary.
He had all the rights.
So we were in Fastiv quite a long time.
Suddenly, the front from the Soviet occupied started to near, as you know, 1943.
And one point, we were moving.
They moved back to Stanislav.
In Stanislav, we were told that we are going to go back home or that they'll take us.
We were already more than a year, a year and a half.
And all those who were a year-- there were rules that Hungarian soldiers who served in the army or in the front for a year, they're exchanging them.
But this didn't apply to labor camps.
We came to Stanislav.
And in Stanislav, we were on the Belvedere Strasse.
This was known that it was a ghetto.
There, I've seen with my own eyes, have heard stories that how Jews wanted to escape, where they-- they murdered the Jews.
And they were-- we have seen also in the houses.
I have seen, myself, a lot of hiding places.
The doors were bloody.
We could have seen that it was going on a massacre there.
Why in Stanislav?
Well, Stanislav was the Hungarian took-- had their headquarters, as they moved back from Kiev, was in Stanislav.
Stanislav, they call it now Ivano-Frankivsk.
So in Stanislav, we were for quite a time.
It wasn't so bad in Stanislav.
So one day, we thought they're taking us back home.
But they didn't.
It happened the opposite.
From Stanislav, the majority took us back to the [NON-ENGLISH], at a place by the name of [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know what it was, we didn't ask too much.
But there were many units like ours.
And there were a lot of partisans there, also.
I didn't see them, but they said there are partisans among non-Jews, Ukrainians, who at the day, are working for the Germans, and at night, they are partisans.
Over there, we had very bad commanders, very big hunger was.
It was in the winter time.
It was in-- in November.
It start to be December.
It was very, very bad.
Now what did we do?
We were working there on the railroad stations.
And they were constantly-- not bombarding.
They had put mines on the railroad stations for the Hungarian.
So they put in first wagon, an open wagon, labor campers.
They shoot one, the first wagon, because to make sure that there will be a mine, it shouldn't explode.
So that they would, right.
And so I was also involved once upon a time-- there was place-- cases where labor campers were killed in front of-- many cases like this.
Over there was very, very bad in this labor camp.
In this labor camp, I was working also there among these on the railroad, harsher work.
We had different commanders.
We were working there for a time.
But it seems that in Budapest, from our unit, those people were very influential.
They have something made arrangement that our unit should be brought home for us.
They had made-- I don't know what kind.
There were among us very influential people among the 200 individuals.
Intellectuals were in Hungarian in society, even as Jews.
Or they had acquaintances.
They had some generals that the fathers, of the mothers, or with bribery.
There was among us [NON-ENGLISH], Jews who didn't consider themselves Jews, but they were in labor camp with us.
So from here, what happened-- one day, it was like in November, they took us back to Stanislav.
Stanislav was the station again that from Stanislav-- actually, we were there in a place they called it [NON-ENGLISH], a place they call it [NON-ENGLISH], not far from [NON-ENGLISH].
These are the names of these places.
Not to explain how we went there, and bombardments, and so on.
But the war was nearing, and nearing, and nearing the Soviet Union.
But we didn't know about it.
We didn't know too much, because no papers, nothing.
Now came back to Stanislav-- so I'm in Stanislav.
And Stanislav was a good life.
Because there was the [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was the place of supply.
From [NON-ENGLISH], the supplies were gone to different places.
So there were wine, there were meats there.
And labor campers were working in the supplies-- delivering from one place to another place where.
I wasn't working, but it was good and all.
We could get clothing there and things like this.
And we were living in-- not in barracks.
We were even living in the headquarters where the ghetto was, in the Belvedere Street in private-- not private houses, but houses.
And we were not so watched.
We were like in a ghetto, but you can move around.
And here--
No, no.
We are not working already.
Here, somehow, they figured out that our unit was expecting that another unit will come, will exchange, and will take us home.
They didn't tell us this, but this was the idea.
That's why they took us.
They took back only our unit.
We are not talking the rest.
The rest is now still in the Soviets' occupied territory behind the fronts.
Some of there are in Hungarian labor camps, inside Hungary.
And some of them in the Carpathian.
And some of them are in Stanislav.
As we are, there are thousands and thousands of labor campers-- not with us, but nearby.
We don't know nothing too much about them.
They are expecting, waiting, you know.
So here in Stanislav, one day they put us in wagons, trains.
Our unit-- we're talking about the 200 people.
And we're going to watch Hungary, going home.
Now I heard this was the first unit in 1944, in January, they took back to Hungary-- maybe later was one or two more units.
I was just plain luck or for some reason our unit.
And what did they do with us?
They bring us back to a place where they call it in southern Hungary, Mohacs.
Mohacs in known from history.
And in Mohacs, it's very nice, good food.
It's in a quarantine.
Three weeks after you will be there, you will be-- they didn't tell us what they do, but we discharged.
And three weeks passed, over there came some photographer.
I had with me from the Stanislav a jacket, a Hungarian jacket, like a labor-- not labor camp, from the army.
And I had a hat.
And I took a jacket and I brought it also back.
And I had it in my suitcase, whatever.
It was in case I'll need some time.
Over there, photographers came.
It was a photographer came, a small one, who wanted to make pictures of those who came back from the labor camp.
They wanted to show their wives, how they look.
They made labor-- and I put on this jacket I made for myself, like I would have been a soldier.
And they didn't care.
And that's why I had a picture like this.
I thought maybe some day I'll need it for identification or something.
Like this, I'll put on like this, I have discharge paper, I'll serve here like someone who served.
I didn't know for what use.
But I had this.
This was 1944, was around January.
After being there for three weeks in Mohacs-- they discharged everyone.
I have a paper, also.
And everyone goes home freely.
Now I was among those who went-- was drafted among from Budapest.
So all of those who were from Budapest or nearby, they went to Budapest.
Where am I going?
I'm going back to Dovhoje.
Now Budapest is completely a different direction.
I came to Budapest also, be there, and discharged, some papers, everything all right, and free.
Everyone is labor camp.
And we were discharged.
And they became free.
So I'm going to Dovhoje, with a thought that I'm going to stay for a while.
Because it was before the holidays of Passover.
And after that, I'll go back to Budapest and see what I can go further from here.
I am discharged.
I am not anymore labor camp.
So I go back to Dovhoje.
Needless to say, it's 1944, March, around 26th.
The Germans are coming into Budapest, to Hungary.
Not no one came into Dovhoje.
But as we heard it.
And the Germans came into some place.
Now I was the only one from my unit who came back to Dovhoje.
There was no one else.
The rest remained in Budapest who were liberated.
I mean it was-- so I'm in Dovhoje.
And needless to say, it's coming the new laws, Hungarian laws about the Jews.
Talking now, I'm going to talk only about Jews from the Carpathia.
I'm not going to talk about other Jews, because-- a curfew became.
No one can got out from town, everyone had to stay.
Now--
My father and my sister was home.
My other brother was somewhere in the army, in the labor camp, in Hungary, in fact.
My younger brother, we didn't know where he is.
He was also some place in labor camp.
But he was probably still near the front in occupied territories.
We didn't have from him writings and nothing.
I was, and my sister, and my father was at home.
And everything was normal until now.
So I was caught here.
I figured out here was a curfew.
And not needless to say that the town crier, with a drum went, he was on hand and making call this announcement.
But the gendarmes, again, weren't all of the same gendarmes remained in Dovhoje.
We knew everyone-- harsh, cruel, brutal people.
And Jews didn't know what to do.
So I figured like this-- I'll move from Dovhoje here.
Even despite the curfew, I'm discharged from the army.
I have a paper.
How can I move?
They'll see that I am Jewish.
It was forbidden to go on a train.
I put on my jacket, my uniform.
And I'll go like a soldier, like Hungarian soldier.
And gendarmes were watching.
I didn't step in in Dovhoje the station.
I went to a nearby station early in the morning, going to Beregszasz.
First, before that, few are coming there-- ID.
I showed my ID.
I'm discharged.
And I'm Hungarian.
They didn't understand too much.
They saw a paper, and they saw that a picture of mine.
They look at it.
It's my picture-- very good.
I'm going further.
Come another station again, the gendarmes are again identifying people.
Nothing-- until I came to a station by the name Bereg, not far from Beregszasz It was about a two hour ride.
I come to there.
Gendarmes are coming up and looking at me.
I was already very much afraid to go in this jacket, because it was belonged to the army.
But they saw-- they started the Hungarian curses, you are Jewish, and took me out from the train.
One wants to hit me, march out from the train, out.
Put me down to wait until they'll come, because they were searching the whole train if there is Jew.
I figured out I'll be in very big trouble.
I started to run from there.
It was some fields.
I run into the small village, Bereg.
There was a Jew by the name of [NON-ENGLISH], that family.
I knew this family, told them the story.
He says, I shouldn't do nothing.
I should be very much careful.
Because they are here very bad gendarmes.
Because I'm going to Beregszasz, I want to see what's in Beregszasz.
So finally comes to what evening.
I took off my jacket.
I was very much afraid.
I had with me-- and I'm going for Beregszasz by foot, which was about an hour.
Come to Beregszasz to my sister.
I had a sister, she had two children.
Oh, she was very much wonder, what are you doing here?
How did you dare to come?
I said, I came here.
I said, you know what's happening here?
I didn't know what's in Beregszasz.
The Germans came here the other day.
And it's a whole thing.
They took the rabbi, and they took from representatives of the town.
There was a rabbi by the name of Hersch-- a very well known, a scholar and a very, very learned man-- ransom.
They want, I don't know how many thousands and thousands of pengos, or dollars, whatever the money.
And they are collecting from every family in Beregszasz where there are a lot of rich people, in order to relieve them.
They have made a price, if the Jewish community will give some--
And she says to me, how did you dare to come here?
What happened?
I told her the story.
I come to Beregszasz I think it, I'll go to Budapest.
No, I saw that things are nothing else.
I'll mingle among Budapest, and then I'll get lost.
I have a discharge papers.
And my sister tells me, I don't think you should dare.
You'll be caught right here at the station.
You'd be caught right on the street here.
You better don't do that.
Because they're sure, and they'll catch you.
Who knows what?
The can shoot you if you have a jacket like this.
I started to think very hardly, because I was a man with daring.
And I think that she may have been right.
Now, how do I return back to Dovhoje?
So I thought-- I thought by train.
So I got over by train.
I don't go-- I go back by foot to Bereg, early in the morning.
No one should see me through the fields.
You can go through the fields there.
I come back.
But I didn't want to go on the station.
It was a small station.
I took off my jacket.
I went to the station.
I come on the train.
No one is there on the train.
The man comes who collects the tickets.
I didn't buy a ticket.
I had some money, pengo.
I believe I had a pair of shoes from Beregszasz, new shoes which I did get before the war.
And I hand it to my sister.
I showed him a pair of shoes.
And I gave him the shoes-- new shoes.
You know, it was very hard to get shoes.
So he let me go on the train.
And he understood that I am somehow like this.
He told me I should sit somewhere in the back of the train, a certain place not far.
Because the gendarmes almost on every station, they're coming up, and they're looking.
At first, they're asking him question, are there some Jews?
So I was a little bit bratty.
Can go on with the story, I came back to Dovhoje.
I didn't go to Dovhoje, I meet to the station.
I came a station before.
It was already somehow late in the afternoon.
It was very much about the gendarmes.
And I managed to go into a non-Jewish house, didn't tell the whole story.
Came back to my home, to my town-- very, very afraid.
So every day are coming up new and new laws, new and new announcement.
Now who made these announcement?
What laws they were?
The gendarmes interpreted the law to their discretion, their discretion.
And the town crier went from corner to corner and on a drum-- he had only one hand.
His name was Feather-- was announcing about Jews, and everything.
So Jews had to put on star, everyone had to make a star by tomorrow, yellow star.
Everyone has to paint on the house a big Magen David-- what do you call it-- a Magen David star.
Jews couldn't get any more rations.
And so it's going on.
How shall I describe you?
The situation in Dovhoje was very, very bad.
But here, you cannot run away.
You cannot go-- what's to go, where to go?
My father and my sister-- people are asking me in Dovhoje, my name is Zoli, what's going to happen?
How is it going to be?
By the curfew was-- we cannot go out only from 8 till 6 o'clock-- only what we must to buy.
And gendarmes are enforcing.
And who wants to see a gendarme?
Because he's hitting.
He's-- So I find myself in a situation like this-- why didn't I run away?
Why didn't I go away?
But I'm here.
My father is sad.
And what can you do?
My sister-- not too much food already.
But in our house, it's still a little bit food.
I decided what's going to be is going to be.
I'm going to beat the rest of them.
Now I'm a strong man.
I'll work some place.
What's going to happen?
But so far, nothing's happen.
We don't see even one German.
There's no Germans here, Hungarians, but harsh, and every day new.
So next day comes out every Jewish household has to bring to the synagogue all his belongings-- not furniture, like linens, linens.
So people bundled up whatever they had.
They were poor people.
But usually those days, girls made their dowry and small things.
You know, they had collected linens from their early childhood, was making embroideries, you know.
In all, they've been very poor.
So we take also our bundle.
The shul-- the synagogue was filled up.
Men are not home.
Only women are at home-- older women, younger women, children.
Children age 16, 17, they didn't go.
And here I find myself a man, a young man in my late 20s.
And everyone is looking at-- toward me.
I've already experienced.
I went to labor camp.
I went there.
I know everything was going on.
Forget about radio and paper, newspaper.
Even there is a newspaper, you're not allowed to read it.
Who was going to see you to read a paper?
And beside this, in their paper is written only what they say.
They forbidden all these things.
All this coming on Jews.
They beating up Jews again.
But still, we don't see one German.
It's only Hungarians.
It comes the last day of Pesach.
The day was the 7th of April.
It could have been on the 14th or 15th of April.
It was on a Saturday.
And we hear-- this was almost three weeks after the Germans are there, three weeks.
We hear that neighbors are coming saying from the other towns, they took away the Jews, the entire community.
I looking-- who took away Jews?
Where, what, for children?
And from [NON-ENGLISH].
How do we hear?
Peasants are coming who are walking there.
They went down there to work and saw it with their own eyes.
The last day was on a Saturday.
They took all the Jews.
Now Dovhoje, they didn't take.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next day, Saturady morning-- Sunday morning, I woke up.
I go to the-- to the pharmacy.
The pharmacist was, by the name of Ilkovitch, Joseph, a very nice man, a charitable man, a smart man.
He didn't have children.
And I ask him, what is it he overhead in the pharmacy came in, and people?
I said to him the following, listen, let's clarify this here.
We'll find here a man who's going to go next town.
And let him talk to someone.
Let him see what happened there.
So he takes a neighbor there, a non-Jew, sends them-- listen, you go to [NON-ENGLISH], about half an hour.
You come back, and report, and tell us what happened there-- what?
And he goes there and finds out the Jews were taken away, taken to the train.
And from the train, they took them away further.
Who?
All the Jews-- not even a single wasn't left in [NON-ENGLISH].
They had a population of at least 60, 80 families.
And where were they taken?
He doesn't know.
But we know that from [NON-ENGLISH] it could be only one direction.
Could go only to Beregszasz-- a small, small train is going.
It couldn't be different.
So they probably took them to-- to-- to that direction.
I mean, there's nothing-- no place else can they go from there.
They took them by train.
If they would take them through Dovhoje, then they goes a different direction.
So it's true.
Now this pharmacist, a nice man-- what are we going to do?
They take us to Israel.
They take us too.
I say to him, I have an idea what to do something.
You know, there are communicable diseases all the time.
And there was from time to time typhoid here, I know the doctor here, a non-Jew by the name of Batory.
I had before him also dealings.
I had before also dealings.
I go to this doctor.
And maybe we'll be able to do something that there is typhoid in town.
And they're not going to take them.
They're going to be afraid.
Usually, with typhoid, they put a-- a red tape around the house.
It's communicable.
They didn't let out no one.
He says it's a good idea.
And Batory was a non-Jew.
But he was quite a nice man.
And I knew him also from before.
I already had with him something like this before when they deported the Jews to communist Podolsk.
He did me also something like this.
